title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read an article about memory and the brain. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Stories We Keep
content.subtitle: Why memory is less reliable than we think
content.text:
Memory is not a recording device. This is the first thing that researchers in cognitive science want the general public to understand, and it is the finding most likely to be resisted. [31]We tend to think of our memories as faithful archives — detailed, accurate, safely stored — when in fact they are more like stories we tell ourselves, reshaped every time we retrieve them, influenced by subsequent experience, emotion, and suggestion.[/31]

The psychologist Elizabeth Loftus spent decades demonstrating the malleability of human memory, conducting experiments in which participants could be led to remember events that had never occurred simply through the power of suggestion. [32]In one famous study, participants who were told — falsely — that a childhood photograph showed them riding in a hot air balloon subsequently reported vivid memories of the event, complete with sensory detail. The memories felt entirely real to those who held them.[/32]

These findings have significant implications for the legal system, which has historically placed considerable weight on eyewitness testimony. [33]A witness who is entirely convinced that they saw something may, in fact, be remembering a reconstructed version of events that has been unconsciously modified by the questions they were asked, the accounts of other witnesses, or simply the passage of time.[/33] Legal reformers have argued that juries should be far more cautious about the reliability of memory-based evidence.

Not all aspects of memory are equally susceptible to distortion. [34]Research suggests that memories with a strong emotional charge — particularly those associated with fear or trauma — are encoded more deeply and retained more reliably than memories of neutral events. The brain appears to prioritise information that carries survival significance.[/34]

However, even highly charged memories are not immune to change. [35]Studies of individuals recalling traumatic events years after they occurred consistently show differences between early and later accounts — not because the individuals are being dishonest, but because memory, even at its most vivid, is a reconstructive rather than a reproductive process.[/35]

[36]Understanding this has practical importance beyond the courtroom. It suggests that the way we discuss memories — with children, with trauma survivors, with witnesses — matters enormously. The language of an interview can become part of the memory itself, shaping what is remembered and how it is experienced.[/36]

questions.question: What mistaken assumption about memory does the article challenge?
options: A) That memories become clearer and more detailed with the passage of time.
B) That emotional events are inherently easier to remember than neutral ones.
C) That our memories function as accurate and stable records of past events.
D) That most people can reliably distinguish between real and imagined memories.
questions.explanation: The text challenges the assumption that memories are 'faithful archives — detailed, accurate, safely stored,' arguing instead that they are reshaped stories.

questions.question: What did Elizabeth Loftus's balloon experiment demonstrate?
options: A) That human beings have a natural tendency to embellish their childhood memories.
B) That people can develop convincingly detailed memories of things that never happened.
C) That childhood memories are more unreliable than memories from adulthood.
D) That photographs are an effective tool for helping people recover lost memories.
questions.explanation: Participants reported 'vivid memories of the event, complete with sensory detail' for something that never happened, showing false memories can feel entirely real.

questions.question: What concern does the article raise about eyewitness testimony in legal cases?
options: A) That witnesses sometimes deliberately alter their accounts to support a particular verdict.
B) That eyewitness accounts are only reliable when given immediately after an event.
C) That judges are not sufficiently trained to assess the quality of witness testimony.
D) That a confident witness may be recounting a memory that has been unconsciously altered.
questions.explanation: A witness 'entirely convinced that they saw something may be remembering a reconstructed version' modified by questions, other accounts, or time — all unconscious changes.

questions.question: What does the brain appear to do with emotionally significant memories?
options: A) It stores them more securely than it stores memories of less important events.
B) It replays them repeatedly over time, making them more detailed in the process.
C) It transfers them from short-term to long-term memory more quickly than other memories.
D) It protects them entirely from the distorting influence of later experience.
questions.explanation: The text states that emotionally charged memories 'are encoded more deeply and retained more reliably,' as the brain prioritises information with survival significance.

questions.question: What do studies of traumatic memory recall show?
options: A) That the initial account given after a traumatic event is always the most accurate version.
B) That traumatic memories are entirely reliable despite the emotional intensity involved.
C) That even vivid memories are reconstructed afresh rather than simply replayed.
D) That individuals who have experienced trauma should not be interviewed as legal witnesses.
questions.explanation: Differences between early and later accounts arise 'because memory, even at its most vivid, is a reconstructive rather than a reproductive process.'

questions.question: What practical importance does the final paragraph identify in understanding memory's reconstructive nature?
options: A) It should cause scientists to stop relying on verbal accounts as research data.
B) It means that the words used when discussing memories with someone can alter what they remember.
C) It demonstrates that written records are always more reliable than human memory.
D) It shows that memory can be significantly improved through specific training techniques.
questions.explanation: The text warns that 'the language of an interview can become part of the memory itself, shaping what is remembered and how it is experienced.'
